item.title: 21/21 - Below PyTorch: Profiling, Compilation, and CUDA Kernel Optimization
item.description:
21-part field guide

AI Infrastructure Engineer: From Silicon to Serving

A 21-part field guide to the hardware, runtimes, distributed systems, serving platforms, data planes, security controls, and operational practices behind production AI.

  1. 01Inside the GPU: From SMs to HBM Without the Hand-Waving
  2. 02Smaller Numbers, Faster Models: Quantization and Batching
  3. 03The Inference Engine Room: vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp
  4. 04The Memory of a Conversation: KV, Prefix Reuse, Speculation, and Throughput
  5. 05Training Across the Fleet: DDP, FSDP, DeepSpeed, and ZeRO
  6. 06The Serving Layer: Triton, vLLM, KServe, Ray Serve, and SGLang
  7. 07Kubernetes Meets GPUs: Containers, Scheduling, and Isolation
  8. 08The Fabric Between GPUs: NCCL, InfiniBand, RoCE, and GPUDirect
  9. 09One Model, Many Accelerators: Multi-GPU and Multi-Node Inference
  10. 10Fine-Tuning Without the Full Bill: LoRA, QLoRA, and PEFT
  11. 11RAG That Survives Production: Embeddings, Retrieval, and Evidence
  12. 12Cache the Right Thing: Prompt, Semantic, and Cost-Aware Reuse
  13. 13Can You Debug a Token? Observability for AI Systems
  14. 14Benchmarking Without Lying: Evals, Load Tests, and A/B Experiments
  15. 15The Router Is Part of the Model: Routing, Hedging, and Fallback
  16. 16Agents Need Infrastructure Too: MCP and Workflow Orchestration
  17. 17From Kafka to Tokens: Streaming Data and Online Inference
  18. 18Assume the Prompt Is Hostile: Security and Guardrails
  19. 19Shipping Models Like Software: CI/CD, MLflow, and Registries
  20. 20The Ground Beneath AI: Linux, Networking, and Storage
  21. 21Below PyTorch: Profiling, Compilation, and CUDA Kernel Optimization

Framework code becomes dispatcher operations, graphs, kernels, memory operations, and synchronization. Optimization begins by proving which layer owns the bottleneck.

The quickest way into this topic is to follow one unit of work and ask where its bytes, authority, and time go. We will build the intuition first, then keep going into capacity math, placement, failure behavior, and the measurements worth putting on an operator dashboard.

The mental model

The useful unit of design is not the library name but the contract between dispatcher/autograd, graph capture/compiler, CUDA runtime, and kernels. Each boundary needs stable identity, bounded resource use, explicit error semantics, and telemetry. Hidden coupling at one boundary usually appears later as tail latency, unreproducible state, or unsafe recovery.

Description: The diagram separates the user-visible contract from state placement, execution, and control. Read it top to bottom. A tuning change in a lower layer is safe only when the upper-layer contract remains true.

What actually happens

The critical path is Python/API -> dispatch -> graph/fusion -> launch -> profile/verify. Some stages may overlap, but correctness dependencies cannot simply be parallelized away. Separate control metadata from the high-volume data plane, preserve deadlines across calls, and make every retry aware of idempotency and remaining budget.

A field note

CUDA work is asynchronous with respect to the host, so naive Python timers often measure launch overhead rather than kernel completion. Synchronize deliberately or use CUDA events. In PyTorch, graph breaks can return execution to eager mode, and a fused kernel can trade launch reduction for register pressure. Verify numerics after every compiler or kernel change, especially across dtypes.

Description: Follow one unit of work from left to right. The lower panel is the accounting model. It is intentionally explicit because unmeasured queueing and data movement are the most common reasons that component benchmarks fail to predict production behavior.

The capacity equation

speedup is bounded by the fraction of time actually improved (Amdahl's law)

Treat this as a model to validate, not a constant to copy. Measure each term on the exact hardware, model revision, input distribution, and concurrency regime. Capacity planning should reserve failure headroom; running permanently at the cliff makes recovery impossible when a replica, link, or dependency disappears.

A worked production example

Start with one representative workload and record an end-to-end baseline. Apply the equation speedup is bounded by the fraction of time actually improved (Amdahl's law) using measured—not advertised—rates. Increase concurrency until the first queue grows, then identify whether dispatcher/autograd, graph capture/compiler, CUDA runtime, or kernels owns that queue. The saturation point and recovery curve are more useful than an isolated peak number.

Run the experiment in at least three regimes: one request for floor latency, a realistic concurrency distribution for normal operation, and controlled overload for backpressure and recovery. A system is not healthy merely because it eventually completes every request. Queue age, deadline misses, quality, and resource recovery all belong in the acceptance criteria.

Description: The timeline identifies where work waits and where it executes. Instrument both sides of every transition so queue time cannot be mistaken for compute time. Compare steady state with the warm-up and recovery periods rather than deleting them from the report.

Placement, topology, and scale

Logical architecture hides physical asymmetry. Two workers can have the same configuration while differing in accelerator generation, NUMA path, network hops, cache warmth, storage locality, or noisy-neighbor pressure. Placement must therefore be expressed as constraints and verified through telemetry.

Description: The two domains are intentionally independent. Local queues contain transient pressure; durable identity lets work move; the fabric is treated as a finite resource. A cross-domain design should say what happens when the fabric is slow, partitioned, or only partially available.

Failure analysis

The triggering event is rarely the entire incident. Cascades occur when a local failure creates retries, retries create more load, and overloaded dependencies become less responsive. Bound attempts, preserve the original deadline, add jitter, and open circuits by route or failure domain rather than disabling an entire platform.

Description: Trace the trigger downward into three distinct consequences. Correctness, performance, and operability require different detection and recovery controls; one generic health check cannot represent all three.

The control loop

Production optimization is a feedback system. Signals must be fresh and correctly scoped; decisions need hysteresis or cooldown; actions need bounds; verification must compare the intended metric without hiding regressions elsewhere. If a controller can add load faster than the system can observe the result, it will oscillate.

Description: A safe controller closes the loop. It does not stop after changing a batch size, replica count, route weight, or precision. It checks quality and SLOs, attributes the outcome, and rolls back when the invariant is violated.

What to measure

  • latency and queue time around dispatcher/autograd
  • capacity and pressure for graph capture/compiler
  • throughput, failures, and retries in CUDA runtime
  • decision reasons emitted by kernels
  • quality, cost, and SLO goodput by workload slice

Always segment these measurements by model revision, workload class, hardware type, and outcome. A fleet-wide average can look healthy while one tenant, long-context bucket, adapter, or accelerator generation is failing.

From laboratory result to production capability

A laboratory result proves that one configuration worked once. A production capability proves that the same contract survives concurrency, skew, partial failure, deployment, and rollback. Record the complete experiment envelope: hardware SKU and topology, driver and runtime versions, model and tokenizer digests, request distribution, warm-up policy, concurrency, precision, and every non-default control. Without that envelope, a performance number is not reproducible evidence.

Separate floor latency, sustainable throughput, and recovery capacity. Floor latency is measured with no queue. Sustainable throughput is the highest rate that keeps queue age and SLO violations bounded over a long run. Recovery capacity is spare work the system can absorb after a replica, link, node, or dependency is lost. These are different numbers. Peak throughput is usually above the sustainable point and says little about safe production capacity.

Roll out in stages. First shadow inputs where policy permits, then canary a narrow workload slice, then increase traffic while comparing quality and operational distributions with the baseline. Make the rollback trigger machine-readable before rollout begins. A rollback that requires an operator to rediscover the previous model, state schema, or runtime image is not a rollback plan.

Debugging order

Debug from the outside inward. Confirm the request identity and deadline, then measure admission and queueing, then state lookup or transfer, then execution, then serialization and downstream delivery. Correlate all five with one trace identity. This order prevents a common mistake: optimizing the most visible kernel while the actual delay is a queue, a copy, a collective, a storage read, or a retry outside the profiler window.

Change one independent variable at a time and retain the raw samples. If a change improves the median but damages the p99, quality, or recovery time, it is not an unconditional improvement. Explain which workload segment benefits and encode that scope in routing or policy instead of applying the change globally.

Design-review checklist

  • Is every artifact and state transition bound to a stable version or digest?
  • Where does work wait, what bounds that queue, and what happens at the bound?
  • Which failures are retryable, and how are deadline and idempotency preserved?
  • Which resource saturates first under representative load?
  • Can operators distinguish correctness failure from overload and dependency failure?
  • Does rollback restore both code and state compatibility?
  • Are sensitive inputs, outputs, credentials, and telemetry scoped and redacted?
  • Has the recovery path been tested under partial failure rather than described only on paper?

Primary and official references

  • PyTorch profiler
  • torch.compile
  • CUDA Best Practices

The takeaway

Framework code becomes dispatcher operations, graphs, kernels, memory operations, and synchronization. Optimization begins by proving which layer owns the bottleneck. The engineering discipline is to make that claim measurable: define the contract, map state and work to real resources, test the failure boundary, and operate a feedback loop that protects correctness before chasing peak throughput.

item.title: 20/21 - The Ground Beneath AI: Linux, Networking, and Storage
item.description:
21-part field guide

AI Infrastructure Engineer: From Silicon to Serving

A 21-part field guide to the hardware, runtimes, distributed systems, serving platforms, data planes, security controls, and operational practices behind production AI.

  1. 01Inside the GPU: From SMs to HBM Without the Hand-Waving
  2. 02Smaller Numbers, Faster Models: Quantization and Batching
  3. 03The Inference Engine Room: vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp
  4. 04The Memory of a Conversation: KV, Prefix Reuse, Speculation, and Throughput
  5. 05Training Across the Fleet: DDP, FSDP, DeepSpeed, and ZeRO
  6. 06The Serving Layer: Triton, vLLM, KServe, Ray Serve, and SGLang
  7. 07Kubernetes Meets GPUs: Containers, Scheduling, and Isolation
  8. 08The Fabric Between GPUs: NCCL, InfiniBand, RoCE, and GPUDirect
  9. 09One Model, Many Accelerators: Multi-GPU and Multi-Node Inference
  10. 10Fine-Tuning Without the Full Bill: LoRA, QLoRA, and PEFT
  11. 11RAG That Survives Production: Embeddings, Retrieval, and Evidence
  12. 12Cache the Right Thing: Prompt, Semantic, and Cost-Aware Reuse
  13. 13Can You Debug a Token? Observability for AI Systems
  14. 14Benchmarking Without Lying: Evals, Load Tests, and A/B Experiments
  15. 15The Router Is Part of the Model: Routing, Hedging, and Fallback
  16. 16Agents Need Infrastructure Too: MCP and Workflow Orchestration
  17. 17From Kafka to Tokens: Streaming Data and Online Inference
  18. 18Assume the Prompt Is Hostile: Security and Guardrails
  19. 19Shipping Models Like Software: CI/CD, MLflow, and Registries
  20. 20The Ground Beneath AI: Linux, Networking, and Storage
  21. 21Below PyTorch: Profiling, Compilation, and CUDA Kernel Optimization

GPU services still depend on processes, virtual memory, NUMA placement, sockets, DNS, filesystems, page cache, and storage queues; weak fundamentals become expensive GPU idle time.

A useful design review for this system begins with a request, follows its state, and refuses to hide waiting time inside a single latency number. We will build the intuition first, then keep going into capacity math, placement, failure behavior, and the measurements worth putting on an operator dashboard.

The mental model

The useful unit of design is not the library name but the contract between process/cgroup, virtual memory/NUMA, TCP/RDMA, and filesystem/block IO. Each boundary needs stable identity, bounded resource use, explicit error semantics, and telemetry. Hidden coupling at one boundary usually appears later as tail latency, unreproducible state, or unsafe recovery.

Description: The diagram separates the user-visible contract from state placement, execution, and control. Read it top to bottom. A tuning change in a lower layer is safe only when the upper-layer contract remains true.

What actually happens

The critical path is read -> page/cache -> DMA -> compute -> write/stream. Some stages may overlap, but correctness dependencies cannot simply be parallelized away. Separate control metadata from the high-volume data plane, preserve deadlines across calls, and make every retry aware of idempotency and remaining budget.

A field note

GPU idle time is often a Linux story. A remote checkpoint can be blocked on DNS, TCP retransmission, page cache, decompression, NUMA traffic, or storage queue depth before CUDA sees a byte. Learn to read process state, cgroups, socket statistics, mount options, block-device latency, and NUMA placement; those tools frequently explain the expensive gap in a GPU trace.

Description: Follow one unit of work from left to right. The lower panel is the accounting model. It is intentionally explicit because unmeasured queueing and data movement are the most common reasons that component benchmarks fail to predict production behavior.

The capacity equation

throughput ceiling is the minimum sustainable rate across pipeline stages

Treat this as a model to validate, not a constant to copy. Measure each term on the exact hardware, model revision, input distribution, and concurrency regime. Capacity planning should reserve failure headroom; running permanently at the cliff makes recovery impossible when a replica, link, or dependency disappears.

A worked production example

Start with one representative workload and record an end-to-end baseline. Apply the equation throughput ceiling is the minimum sustainable rate across pipeline stages using measured—not advertised—rates. Increase concurrency until the first queue grows, then identify whether process/cgroup, virtual memory/NUMA, TCP/RDMA, or filesystem/block IO owns that queue. The saturation point and recovery curve are more useful than an isolated peak number.

Run the experiment in at least three regimes: one request for floor latency, a realistic concurrency distribution for normal operation, and controlled overload for backpressure and recovery. A system is not healthy merely because it eventually completes every request. Queue age, deadline misses, quality, and resource recovery all belong in the acceptance criteria.

Description: The timeline identifies where work waits and where it executes. Instrument both sides of every transition so queue time cannot be mistaken for compute time. Compare steady state with the warm-up and recovery periods rather than deleting them from the report.

Placement, topology, and scale

Logical architecture hides physical asymmetry. Two workers can have the same configuration while differing in accelerator generation, NUMA path, network hops, cache warmth, storage locality, or noisy-neighbor pressure. Placement must therefore be expressed as constraints and verified through telemetry.

Description: The two domains are intentionally independent. Local queues contain transient pressure; durable identity lets work move; the fabric is treated as a finite resource. A cross-domain design should say what happens when the fabric is slow, partitioned, or only partially available.

Failure analysis

The triggering event is rarely the entire incident. Cascades occur when a local failure creates retries, retries create more load, and overloaded dependencies become less responsive. Bound attempts, preserve the original deadline, add jitter, and open circuits by route or failure domain rather than disabling an entire platform.

Description: Trace the trigger downward into three distinct consequences. Correctness, performance, and operability require different detection and recovery controls; one generic health check cannot represent all three.

The control loop

Production optimization is a feedback system. Signals must be fresh and correctly scoped; decisions need hysteresis or cooldown; actions need bounds; verification must compare the intended metric without hiding regressions elsewhere. If a controller can add load faster than the system can observe the result, it will oscillate.

Description: A safe controller closes the loop. It does not stop after changing a batch size, replica count, route weight, or precision. It checks quality and SLOs, attributes the outcome, and rolls back when the invariant is violated.

What to measure

  • latency and queue time around process/cgroup
  • capacity and pressure for virtual memory/NUMA
  • throughput, failures, and retries in TCP/RDMA
  • decision reasons emitted by filesystem/block IO
  • quality, cost, and SLO goodput by workload slice

Always segment these measurements by model revision, workload class, hardware type, and outcome. A fleet-wide average can look healthy while one tenant, long-context bucket, adapter, or accelerator generation is failing.

From laboratory result to production capability

A laboratory result proves that one configuration worked once. A production capability proves that the same contract survives concurrency, skew, partial failure, deployment, and rollback. Record the complete experiment envelope: hardware SKU and topology, driver and runtime versions, model and tokenizer digests, request distribution, warm-up policy, concurrency, precision, and every non-default control. Without that envelope, a performance number is not reproducible evidence.

Separate floor latency, sustainable throughput, and recovery capacity. Floor latency is measured with no queue. Sustainable throughput is the highest rate that keeps queue age and SLO violations bounded over a long run. Recovery capacity is spare work the system can absorb after a replica, link, node, or dependency is lost. These are different numbers. Peak throughput is usually above the sustainable point and says little about safe production capacity.

Roll out in stages. First shadow inputs where policy permits, then canary a narrow workload slice, then increase traffic while comparing quality and operational distributions with the baseline. Make the rollback trigger machine-readable before rollout begins. A rollback that requires an operator to rediscover the previous model, state schema, or runtime image is not a rollback plan.

Debugging order

Debug from the outside inward. Confirm the request identity and deadline, then measure admission and queueing, then state lookup or transfer, then execution, then serialization and downstream delivery. Correlate all five with one trace identity. This order prevents a common mistake: optimizing the most visible kernel while the actual delay is a queue, a copy, a collective, a storage read, or a retry outside the profiler window.

Change one independent variable at a time and retain the raw samples. If a change improves the median but damages the p99, quality, or recovery time, it is not an unconditional improvement. Explain which workload segment benefits and encode that scope in routing or policy instead of applying the change globally.

Design-review checklist

  • Is every artifact and state transition bound to a stable version or digest?
  • Where does work wait, what bounds that queue, and what happens at the bound?
  • Which failures are retryable, and how are deadline and idempotency preserved?
  • Which resource saturates first under representative load?
  • Can operators distinguish correctness failure from overload and dependency failure?
  • Does rollback restore both code and state compatibility?
  • Are sensitive inputs, outputs, credentials, and telemetry scoped and redacted?
  • Has the recovery path been tested under partial failure rather than described only on paper?

Primary and official references

  • Linux kernel documentation
  • Kubernetes resource management
  • GPUDirect Storage

The takeaway

GPU services still depend on processes, virtual memory, NUMA placement, sockets, DNS, filesystems, page cache, and storage queues; weak fundamentals become expensive GPU idle time. The engineering discipline is to make that claim measurable: define the contract, map state and work to real resources, test the failure boundary, and operate a feedback loop that protects correctness before chasing peak throughput.

item.title: 19/21 - Shipping Models Like Software: CI/CD, MLflow, and Registries
item.description:
21-part field guide

AI Infrastructure Engineer: From Silicon to Serving

A 21-part field guide to the hardware, runtimes, distributed systems, serving platforms, data planes, security controls, and operational practices behind production AI.

  1. 01Inside the GPU: From SMs to HBM Without the Hand-Waving
  2. 02Smaller Numbers, Faster Models: Quantization and Batching
  3. 03The Inference Engine Room: vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp
  4. 04The Memory of a Conversation: KV, Prefix Reuse, Speculation, and Throughput
  5. 05Training Across the Fleet: DDP, FSDP, DeepSpeed, and ZeRO
  6. 06The Serving Layer: Triton, vLLM, KServe, Ray Serve, and SGLang
  7. 07Kubernetes Meets GPUs: Containers, Scheduling, and Isolation
  8. 08The Fabric Between GPUs: NCCL, InfiniBand, RoCE, and GPUDirect
  9. 09One Model, Many Accelerators: Multi-GPU and Multi-Node Inference
  10. 10Fine-Tuning Without the Full Bill: LoRA, QLoRA, and PEFT
  11. 11RAG That Survives Production: Embeddings, Retrieval, and Evidence
  12. 12Cache the Right Thing: Prompt, Semantic, and Cost-Aware Reuse
  13. 13Can You Debug a Token? Observability for AI Systems
  14. 14Benchmarking Without Lying: Evals, Load Tests, and A/B Experiments
  15. 15The Router Is Part of the Model: Routing, Hedging, and Fallback
  16. 16Agents Need Infrastructure Too: MCP and Workflow Orchestration
  17. 17From Kafka to Tokens: Streaming Data and Online Inference
  18. 18Assume the Prompt Is Hostile: Security and Guardrails
  19. 19Shipping Models Like Software: CI/CD, MLflow, and Registries
  20. 20The Ground Beneath AI: Linux, Networking, and Storage
  21. 21Below PyTorch: Profiling, Compilation, and CUDA Kernel Optimization

A model release is an immutable bundle of weights, code, tokenizer, runtime, data lineage, evaluation, policy, and deployment configuration promoted by evidence.

Start with the boundary, not the brand name. Once the contract is explicit, the implementation choices become much easier to compare. We will build the intuition first, then keep going into capacity math, placement, failure behavior, and the measurements worth putting on an operator dashboard.

The mental model

The useful unit of design is not the library name but the contract between experiment tracking, artifact store, registry, and deployment controller. Each boundary needs stable identity, bounded resource use, explicit error semantics, and telemetry. Hidden coupling at one boundary usually appears later as tail latency, unreproducible state, or unsafe recovery.

Description: The diagram separates the user-visible contract from state placement, execution, and control. Read it top to bottom. A tuning change in a lower layer is safe only when the upper-layer contract remains true.

What actually happens

The critical path is train -> evaluate -> register -> stage -> canary/promote/rollback. Some stages may overlap, but correctness dependencies cannot simply be parallelized away. Separate control metadata from the high-volume data plane, preserve deadlines across calls, and make every retry aware of idempotency and remaining budget.

A field note

A registry alias such as champion is useful precisely because it is mutable; the artifact it points to should not be. Record the resolved digest at deployment and in every inference trace. Rebuilding an image from the same branch name is not reproducibility, and rolling back application code without restoring tokenizer, schema, or feature compatibility is not a complete rollback.

Description: Follow one unit of work from left to right. The lower panel is the accounting model. It is intentionally explicit because unmeasured queueing and data movement are the most common reasons that component benchmarks fail to predict production behavior.

The capacity equation

promotion requires quality gates + operational gates + governance gates

Treat this as a model to validate, not a constant to copy. Measure each term on the exact hardware, model revision, input distribution, and concurrency regime. Capacity planning should reserve failure headroom; running permanently at the cliff makes recovery impossible when a replica, link, or dependency disappears.

A worked production example

Start with one representative workload and record an end-to-end baseline. Apply the equation promotion requires quality gates + operational gates + governance gates using measured—not advertised—rates. Increase concurrency until the first queue grows, then identify whether experiment tracking, artifact store, registry, or deployment controller owns that queue. The saturation point and recovery curve are more useful than an isolated peak number.

Run the experiment in at least three regimes: one request for floor latency, a realistic concurrency distribution for normal operation, and controlled overload for backpressure and recovery. A system is not healthy merely because it eventually completes every request. Queue age, deadline misses, quality, and resource recovery all belong in the acceptance criteria.

Description: The timeline identifies where work waits and where it executes. Instrument both sides of every transition so queue time cannot be mistaken for compute time. Compare steady state with the warm-up and recovery periods rather than deleting them from the report.

Placement, topology, and scale

Logical architecture hides physical asymmetry. Two workers can have the same configuration while differing in accelerator generation, NUMA path, network hops, cache warmth, storage locality, or noisy-neighbor pressure. Placement must therefore be expressed as constraints and verified through telemetry.

Description: The two domains are intentionally independent. Local queues contain transient pressure; durable identity lets work move; the fabric is treated as a finite resource. A cross-domain design should say what happens when the fabric is slow, partitioned, or only partially available.

Failure analysis

The triggering event is rarely the entire incident. Cascades occur when a local failure creates retries, retries create more load, and overloaded dependencies become less responsive. Bound attempts, preserve the original deadline, add jitter, and open circuits by route or failure domain rather than disabling an entire platform.

Description: Trace the trigger downward into three distinct consequences. Correctness, performance, and operability require different detection and recovery controls; one generic health check cannot represent all three.

The control loop

Production optimization is a feedback system. Signals must be fresh and correctly scoped; decisions need hysteresis or cooldown; actions need bounds; verification must compare the intended metric without hiding regressions elsewhere. If a controller can add load faster than the system can observe the result, it will oscillate.

Description: A safe controller closes the loop. It does not stop after changing a batch size, replica count, route weight, or precision. It checks quality and SLOs, attributes the outcome, and rolls back when the invariant is violated.

What to measure

  • latency and queue time around experiment tracking
  • capacity and pressure for artifact store
  • throughput, failures, and retries in registry
  • decision reasons emitted by deployment controller
  • quality, cost, and SLO goodput by workload slice

Always segment these measurements by model revision, workload class, hardware type, and outcome. A fleet-wide average can look healthy while one tenant, long-context bucket, adapter, or accelerator generation is failing.

From laboratory result to production capability

A laboratory result proves that one configuration worked once. A production capability proves that the same contract survives concurrency, skew, partial failure, deployment, and rollback. Record the complete experiment envelope: hardware SKU and topology, driver and runtime versions, model and tokenizer digests, request distribution, warm-up policy, concurrency, precision, and every non-default control. Without that envelope, a performance number is not reproducible evidence.

Separate floor latency, sustainable throughput, and recovery capacity. Floor latency is measured with no queue. Sustainable throughput is the highest rate that keeps queue age and SLO violations bounded over a long run. Recovery capacity is spare work the system can absorb after a replica, link, node, or dependency is lost. These are different numbers. Peak throughput is usually above the sustainable point and says little about safe production capacity.

Roll out in stages. First shadow inputs where policy permits, then canary a narrow workload slice, then increase traffic while comparing quality and operational distributions with the baseline. Make the rollback trigger machine-readable before rollout begins. A rollback that requires an operator to rediscover the previous model, state schema, or runtime image is not a rollback plan.

Debugging order

Debug from the outside inward. Confirm the request identity and deadline, then measure admission and queueing, then state lookup or transfer, then execution, then serialization and downstream delivery. Correlate all five with one trace identity. This order prevents a common mistake: optimizing the most visible kernel while the actual delay is a queue, a copy, a collective, a storage read, or a retry outside the profiler window.

Change one independent variable at a time and retain the raw samples. If a change improves the median but damages the p99, quality, or recovery time, it is not an unconditional improvement. Explain which workload segment benefits and encode that scope in routing or policy instead of applying the change globally.

Design-review checklist

  • Is every artifact and state transition bound to a stable version or digest?
  • Where does work wait, what bounds that queue, and what happens at the bound?
  • Which failures are retryable, and how are deadline and idempotency preserved?
  • Which resource saturates first under representative load?
  • Can operators distinguish correctness failure from overload and dependency failure?
  • Does rollback restore both code and state compatibility?
  • Are sensitive inputs, outputs, credentials, and telemetry scoped and redacted?
  • Has the recovery path been tested under partial failure rather than described only on paper?

Primary and official references

  • MLflow Model Registry
  • MLflow Models
  • SLSA specification

The takeaway

A model release is an immutable bundle of weights, code, tokenizer, runtime, data lineage, evaluation, policy, and deployment configuration promoted by evidence. The engineering discipline is to make that claim measurable: define the contract, map state and work to real resources, test the failure boundary, and operate a feedback loop that protects correctness before chasing peak throughput.

item.title: 18/21 - Assume the Prompt Is Hostile: Security and Guardrails
item.description:
21-part field guide

AI Infrastructure Engineer: From Silicon to Serving

A 21-part field guide to the hardware, runtimes, distributed systems, serving platforms, data planes, security controls, and operational practices behind production AI.

  1. 01Inside the GPU: From SMs to HBM Without the Hand-Waving
  2. 02Smaller Numbers, Faster Models: Quantization and Batching
  3. 03The Inference Engine Room: vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp
  4. 04The Memory of a Conversation: KV, Prefix Reuse, Speculation, and Throughput
  5. 05Training Across the Fleet: DDP, FSDP, DeepSpeed, and ZeRO
  6. 06The Serving Layer: Triton, vLLM, KServe, Ray Serve, and SGLang
  7. 07Kubernetes Meets GPUs: Containers, Scheduling, and Isolation
  8. 08The Fabric Between GPUs: NCCL, InfiniBand, RoCE, and GPUDirect
  9. 09One Model, Many Accelerators: Multi-GPU and Multi-Node Inference
  10. 10Fine-Tuning Without the Full Bill: LoRA, QLoRA, and PEFT
  11. 11RAG That Survives Production: Embeddings, Retrieval, and Evidence
  12. 12Cache the Right Thing: Prompt, Semantic, and Cost-Aware Reuse
  13. 13Can You Debug a Token? Observability for AI Systems
  14. 14Benchmarking Without Lying: Evals, Load Tests, and A/B Experiments
  15. 15The Router Is Part of the Model: Routing, Hedging, and Fallback
  16. 16Agents Need Infrastructure Too: MCP and Workflow Orchestration
  17. 17From Kafka to Tokens: Streaming Data and Online Inference
  18. 18Assume the Prompt Is Hostile: Security and Guardrails
  19. 19Shipping Models Like Software: CI/CD, MLflow, and Registries
  20. 20The Ground Beneath AI: Linux, Networking, and Storage
  21. 21Below PyTorch: Profiling, Compilation, and CUDA Kernel Optimization

Prompt injection cannot be solved by asking the same model to ignore attacks. Secure systems separate instructions from untrusted data and enforce authority outside the model.

The vocabulary is dense, but the engineering question is concrete: what state lives where, who may change it, and what becomes the bottleneck first? We will build the intuition first, then keep going into capacity math, placement, failure behavior, and the measurements worth putting on an operator dashboard.

The mental model

The useful unit of design is not the library name but the contract between identity/policy, untrusted-content boundary, tool broker, and output guard. Each boundary needs stable identity, bounded resource use, explicit error semantics, and telemetry. Hidden coupling at one boundary usually appears later as tail latency, unreproducible state, or unsafe recovery.

Description: The diagram separates the user-visible contract from state placement, execution, and control. Read it top to bottom. A tuning change in a lower layer is safe only when the upper-layer contract remains true.

What actually happens

The critical path is classify source -> constrain context -> authorize action -> validate output -> audit. Some stages may overlap, but correctness dependencies cannot simply be parallelized away. Separate control metadata from the high-volume data plane, preserve deadlines across calls, and make every retry aware of idempotency and remaining budget.

A field note

Indirect prompt injection is the uncomfortable case: the malicious instruction sits in a document, web page, email, code comment, or retrieved chunk that the user legitimately asked the model to read. A classifier can help, but authority must still be enforced outside the model. Separate untrusted content from control instructions and keep high-impact tools behind deterministic policy and approval.

Description: Follow one unit of work from left to right. The lower panel is the accounting model. It is intentionally explicit because unmeasured queueing and data movement are the most common reasons that component benchmarks fail to predict production behavior.

The capacity equation

risk = likelihood * impact; model confidence is not authorization

Treat this as a model to validate, not a constant to copy. Measure each term on the exact hardware, model revision, input distribution, and concurrency regime. Capacity planning should reserve failure headroom; running permanently at the cliff makes recovery impossible when a replica, link, or dependency disappears.

A worked production example

Start with one representative workload and record an end-to-end baseline. Apply the equation risk = likelihood * impact; model confidence is not authorization using measured—not advertised—rates. Increase concurrency until the first queue grows, then identify whether identity/policy, untrusted-content boundary, tool broker, or output guard owns that queue. The saturation point and recovery curve are more useful than an isolated peak number.

Run the experiment in at least three regimes: one request for floor latency, a realistic concurrency distribution for normal operation, and controlled overload for backpressure and recovery. A system is not healthy merely because it eventually completes every request. Queue age, deadline misses, quality, and resource recovery all belong in the acceptance criteria.

Description: The timeline identifies where work waits and where it executes. Instrument both sides of every transition so queue time cannot be mistaken for compute time. Compare steady state with the warm-up and recovery periods rather than deleting them from the report.

Placement, topology, and scale

Logical architecture hides physical asymmetry. Two workers can have the same configuration while differing in accelerator generation, NUMA path, network hops, cache warmth, storage locality, or noisy-neighbor pressure. Placement must therefore be expressed as constraints and verified through telemetry.

Description: The two domains are intentionally independent. Local queues contain transient pressure; durable identity lets work move; the fabric is treated as a finite resource. A cross-domain design should say what happens when the fabric is slow, partitioned, or only partially available.

Failure analysis

The triggering event is rarely the entire incident. Cascades occur when a local failure creates retries, retries create more load, and overloaded dependencies become less responsive. Bound attempts, preserve the original deadline, add jitter, and open circuits by route or failure domain rather than disabling an entire platform.

Description: Trace the trigger downward into three distinct consequences. Correctness, performance, and operability require different detection and recovery controls; one generic health check cannot represent all three.

The control loop

Production optimization is a feedback system. Signals must be fresh and correctly scoped; decisions need hysteresis or cooldown; actions need bounds; verification must compare the intended metric without hiding regressions elsewhere. If a controller can add load faster than the system can observe the result, it will oscillate.

Description: A safe controller closes the loop. It does not stop after changing a batch size, replica count, route weight, or precision. It checks quality and SLOs, attributes the outcome, and rolls back when the invariant is violated.

What to measure

  • latency and queue time around identity/policy
  • capacity and pressure for untrusted-content boundary
  • throughput, failures, and retries in tool broker
  • decision reasons emitted by output guard
  • quality, cost, and SLO goodput by workload slice

Always segment these measurements by model revision, workload class, hardware type, and outcome. A fleet-wide average can look healthy while one tenant, long-context bucket, adapter, or accelerator generation is failing.

From laboratory result to production capability

A laboratory result proves that one configuration worked once. A production capability proves that the same contract survives concurrency, skew, partial failure, deployment, and rollback. Record the complete experiment envelope: hardware SKU and topology, driver and runtime versions, model and tokenizer digests, request distribution, warm-up policy, concurrency, precision, and every non-default control. Without that envelope, a performance number is not reproducible evidence.

Separate floor latency, sustainable throughput, and recovery capacity. Floor latency is measured with no queue. Sustainable throughput is the highest rate that keeps queue age and SLO violations bounded over a long run. Recovery capacity is spare work the system can absorb after a replica, link, node, or dependency is lost. These are different numbers. Peak throughput is usually above the sustainable point and says little about safe production capacity.

Roll out in stages. First shadow inputs where policy permits, then canary a narrow workload slice, then increase traffic while comparing quality and operational distributions with the baseline. Make the rollback trigger machine-readable before rollout begins. A rollback that requires an operator to rediscover the previous model, state schema, or runtime image is not a rollback plan.

Debugging order

Debug from the outside inward. Confirm the request identity and deadline, then measure admission and queueing, then state lookup or transfer, then execution, then serialization and downstream delivery. Correlate all five with one trace identity. This order prevents a common mistake: optimizing the most visible kernel while the actual delay is a queue, a copy, a collective, a storage read, or a retry outside the profiler window.

Change one independent variable at a time and retain the raw samples. If a change improves the median but damages the p99, quality, or recovery time, it is not an unconditional improvement. Explain which workload segment benefits and encode that scope in routing or policy instead of applying the change globally.

Design-review checklist

  • Is every artifact and state transition bound to a stable version or digest?
  • Where does work wait, what bounds that queue, and what happens at the bound?
  • Which failures are retryable, and how are deadline and idempotency preserved?
  • Which resource saturates first under representative load?
  • Can operators distinguish correctness failure from overload and dependency failure?
  • Does rollback restore both code and state compatibility?
  • Are sensitive inputs, outputs, credentials, and telemetry scoped and redacted?
  • Has the recovery path been tested under partial failure rather than described only on paper?

Primary and official references

  • OWASP Prompt Injection Prevention
  • OWASP MCP Security
  • NIST Generative AI Profile

The takeaway

Prompt injection cannot be solved by asking the same model to ignore attacks. Secure systems separate instructions from untrusted data and enforce authority outside the model. The engineering discipline is to make that claim measurable: define the contract, map state and work to real resources, test the failure boundary, and operate a feedback loop that protects correctness before chasing peak throughput.

item.title: 17/21 - From Kafka to Tokens: Streaming Data and Online Inference
item.description:
21-part field guide

AI Infrastructure Engineer: From Silicon to Serving

A 21-part field guide to the hardware, runtimes, distributed systems, serving platforms, data planes, security controls, and operational practices behind production AI.

  1. 01Inside the GPU: From SMs to HBM Without the Hand-Waving
  2. 02Smaller Numbers, Faster Models: Quantization and Batching
  3. 03The Inference Engine Room: vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp
  4. 04The Memory of a Conversation: KV, Prefix Reuse, Speculation, and Throughput
  5. 05Training Across the Fleet: DDP, FSDP, DeepSpeed, and ZeRO
  6. 06The Serving Layer: Triton, vLLM, KServe, Ray Serve, and SGLang
  7. 07Kubernetes Meets GPUs: Containers, Scheduling, and Isolation
  8. 08The Fabric Between GPUs: NCCL, InfiniBand, RoCE, and GPUDirect
  9. 09One Model, Many Accelerators: Multi-GPU and Multi-Node Inference
  10. 10Fine-Tuning Without the Full Bill: LoRA, QLoRA, and PEFT
  11. 11RAG That Survives Production: Embeddings, Retrieval, and Evidence
  12. 12Cache the Right Thing: Prompt, Semantic, and Cost-Aware Reuse
  13. 13Can You Debug a Token? Observability for AI Systems
  14. 14Benchmarking Without Lying: Evals, Load Tests, and A/B Experiments
  15. 15The Router Is Part of the Model: Routing, Hedging, and Fallback
  16. 16Agents Need Infrastructure Too: MCP and Workflow Orchestration
  17. 17From Kafka to Tokens: Streaming Data and Online Inference
  18. 18Assume the Prompt Is Hostile: Security and Guardrails
  19. 19Shipping Models Like Software: CI/CD, MLflow, and Registries
  20. 20The Ground Beneath AI: Linux, Networking, and Storage
  21. 21Below PyTorch: Profiling, Compilation, and CUDA Kernel Optimization

Streaming inference couples an ordered durable log with model execution whose latency, retries, state, and cost differ radically from ordinary record transforms.

I find this easier to reason about when the product promise and the machine mechanism are kept in the same picture. We will build the intuition first, then keep going into capacity math, placement, failure behavior, and the measurements worth putting on an operator dashboard.

The mental model

The useful unit of design is not the library name but the contract between producer, partitioned log, consumer group, and inference sink. Each boundary needs stable identity, bounded resource use, explicit error semantics, and telemetry. Hidden coupling at one boundary usually appears later as tail latency, unreproducible state, or unsafe recovery.

Description: The diagram separates the user-visible contract from state placement, execution, and control. Read it top to bottom. A tuning change in a lower layer is safe only when the upper-layer contract remains true.

What actually happens

The critical path is publish -> partition -> consume -> infer -> commit/output. Some stages may overlap, but correctness dependencies cannot simply be parallelized away. Separate control metadata from the high-volume data plane, preserve deadlines across calls, and make every retry aware of idempotency and remaining budget.

A field note

Kafka ordering is per partition, not global. Consumer-group parallelism is bounded by useful partitions, and committing an offset is not atomic with an external model call or database write. Assume a record can be seen again. Stable event IDs and idempotent sinks turn at-least-once delivery from an incident into an ordinary code path.

Description: Follow one unit of work from left to right. The lower panel is the accounting model. It is intentionally explicit because unmeasured queueing and data movement are the most common reasons that component benchmarks fail to predict production behavior.

The capacity equation

parallel consumers <= useful partitions per consumer group

Treat this as a model to validate, not a constant to copy. Measure each term on the exact hardware, model revision, input distribution, and concurrency regime. Capacity planning should reserve failure headroom; running permanently at the cliff makes recovery impossible when a replica, link, or dependency disappears.

A worked production example

Start with one representative workload and record an end-to-end baseline. Apply the equation parallel consumers <= useful partitions per consumer group using measured—not advertised—rates. Increase concurrency until the first queue grows, then identify whether producer, partitioned log, consumer group, or inference sink owns that queue. The saturation point and recovery curve are more useful than an isolated peak number.

Run the experiment in at least three regimes: one request for floor latency, a realistic concurrency distribution for normal operation, and controlled overload for backpressure and recovery. A system is not healthy merely because it eventually completes every request. Queue age, deadline misses, quality, and resource recovery all belong in the acceptance criteria.

Description: The timeline identifies where work waits and where it executes. Instrument both sides of every transition so queue time cannot be mistaken for compute time. Compare steady state with the warm-up and recovery periods rather than deleting them from the report.

Placement, topology, and scale

Logical architecture hides physical asymmetry. Two workers can have the same configuration while differing in accelerator generation, NUMA path, network hops, cache warmth, storage locality, or noisy-neighbor pressure. Placement must therefore be expressed as constraints and verified through telemetry.

Description: The two domains are intentionally independent. Local queues contain transient pressure; durable identity lets work move; the fabric is treated as a finite resource. A cross-domain design should say what happens when the fabric is slow, partitioned, or only partially available.

Failure analysis

The triggering event is rarely the entire incident. Cascades occur when a local failure creates retries, retries create more load, and overloaded dependencies become less responsive. Bound attempts, preserve the original deadline, add jitter, and open circuits by route or failure domain rather than disabling an entire platform.

Description: Trace the trigger downward into three distinct consequences. Correctness, performance, and operability require different detection and recovery controls; one generic health check cannot represent all three.

The control loop

Production optimization is a feedback system. Signals must be fresh and correctly scoped; decisions need hysteresis or cooldown; actions need bounds; verification must compare the intended metric without hiding regressions elsewhere. If a controller can add load faster than the system can observe the result, it will oscillate.

Description: A safe controller closes the loop. It does not stop after changing a batch size, replica count, route weight, or precision. It checks quality and SLOs, attributes the outcome, and rolls back when the invariant is violated.

What to measure

  • latency and queue time around producer
  • capacity and pressure for partitioned log
  • throughput, failures, and retries in consumer group
  • decision reasons emitted by inference sink
  • quality, cost, and SLO goodput by workload slice

Always segment these measurements by model revision, workload class, hardware type, and outcome. A fleet-wide average can look healthy while one tenant, long-context bucket, adapter, or accelerator generation is failing.

From laboratory result to production capability

A laboratory result proves that one configuration worked once. A production capability proves that the same contract survives concurrency, skew, partial failure, deployment, and rollback. Record the complete experiment envelope: hardware SKU and topology, driver and runtime versions, model and tokenizer digests, request distribution, warm-up policy, concurrency, precision, and every non-default control. Without that envelope, a performance number is not reproducible evidence.

Separate floor latency, sustainable throughput, and recovery capacity. Floor latency is measured with no queue. Sustainable throughput is the highest rate that keeps queue age and SLO violations bounded over a long run. Recovery capacity is spare work the system can absorb after a replica, link, node, or dependency is lost. These are different numbers. Peak throughput is usually above the sustainable point and says little about safe production capacity.

Roll out in stages. First shadow inputs where policy permits, then canary a narrow workload slice, then increase traffic while comparing quality and operational distributions with the baseline. Make the rollback trigger machine-readable before rollout begins. A rollback that requires an operator to rediscover the previous model, state schema, or runtime image is not a rollback plan.

Debugging order

Debug from the outside inward. Confirm the request identity and deadline, then measure admission and queueing, then state lookup or transfer, then execution, then serialization and downstream delivery. Correlate all five with one trace identity. This order prevents a common mistake: optimizing the most visible kernel while the actual delay is a queue, a copy, a collective, a storage read, or a retry outside the profiler window.

Change one independent variable at a time and retain the raw samples. If a change improves the median but damages the p99, quality, or recovery time, it is not an unconditional improvement. Explain which workload segment benefits and encode that scope in routing or policy instead of applying the change globally.

Design-review checklist

  • Is every artifact and state transition bound to a stable version or digest?
  • Where does work wait, what bounds that queue, and what happens at the bound?
  • Which failures are retryable, and how are deadline and idempotency preserved?
  • Which resource saturates first under representative load?
  • Can operators distinguish correctness failure from overload and dependency failure?
  • Does rollback restore both code and state compatibility?
  • Are sensitive inputs, outputs, credentials, and telemetry scoped and redacted?
  • Has the recovery path been tested under partial failure rather than described only on paper?

Primary and official references

  • Apache Kafka design
  • Kafka consumer groups
  • OpenTelemetry messaging conventions

The takeaway

Streaming inference couples an ordered durable log with model execution whose latency, retries, state, and cost differ radically from ordinary record transforms. The engineering discipline is to make that claim measurable: define the contract, map state and work to real resources, test the failure boundary, and operate a feedback loop that protects correctness before chasing peak throughput.

item.title: 16/21 - Agents Need Infrastructure Too: MCP and Workflow Orchestration
item.description:
21-part field guide

AI Infrastructure Engineer: From Silicon to Serving

A 21-part field guide to the hardware, runtimes, distributed systems, serving platforms, data planes, security controls, and operational practices behind production AI.

  1. 01Inside the GPU: From SMs to HBM Without the Hand-Waving
  2. 02Smaller Numbers, Faster Models: Quantization and Batching
  3. 03The Inference Engine Room: vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp
  4. 04The Memory of a Conversation: KV, Prefix Reuse, Speculation, and Throughput
  5. 05Training Across the Fleet: DDP, FSDP, DeepSpeed, and ZeRO
  6. 06The Serving Layer: Triton, vLLM, KServe, Ray Serve, and SGLang
  7. 07Kubernetes Meets GPUs: Containers, Scheduling, and Isolation
  8. 08The Fabric Between GPUs: NCCL, InfiniBand, RoCE, and GPUDirect
  9. 09One Model, Many Accelerators: Multi-GPU and Multi-Node Inference
  10. 10Fine-Tuning Without the Full Bill: LoRA, QLoRA, and PEFT
  11. 11RAG That Survives Production: Embeddings, Retrieval, and Evidence
  12. 12Cache the Right Thing: Prompt, Semantic, and Cost-Aware Reuse
  13. 13Can You Debug a Token? Observability for AI Systems
  14. 14Benchmarking Without Lying: Evals, Load Tests, and A/B Experiments
  15. 15The Router Is Part of the Model: Routing, Hedging, and Fallback
  16. 16Agents Need Infrastructure Too: MCP and Workflow Orchestration
  17. 17From Kafka to Tokens: Streaming Data and Online Inference
  18. 18Assume the Prompt Is Hostile: Security and Guardrails
  19. 19Shipping Models Like Software: CI/CD, MLflow, and Registries
  20. 20The Ground Beneath AI: Linux, Networking, and Storage
  21. 21Below PyTorch: Profiling, Compilation, and CUDA Kernel Optimization

An agent control plane governs identity, tool discovery, intent, approval, execution, receipts, and resumable state while leaving reasoning inside the chosen runtime.

The quickest way into this topic is to follow one unit of work and ask where its bytes, authority, and time go. We will build the intuition first, then keep going into capacity math, placement, failure behavior, and the measurements worth putting on an operator dashboard.

The mental model

The useful unit of design is not the library name but the contract between agent identity, MCP registry, policy engine, and durable workflow. Each boundary needs stable identity, bounded resource use, explicit error semantics, and telemetry. Hidden coupling at one boundary usually appears later as tail latency, unreproducible state, or unsafe recovery.

Description: The diagram separates the user-visible contract from state placement, execution, and control. Read it top to bottom. A tuning change in a lower layer is safe only when the upper-layer contract remains true.

What actually happens

The critical path is authenticate -> plan -> authorize tool -> execute -> record/resume. Some stages may overlap, but correctness dependencies cannot simply be parallelized away. Separate control metadata from the high-volume data plane, preserve deadlines across calls, and make every retry aware of idempotency and remaining budget.

A field note

MCP standardizes how a client discovers and invokes tools, resources, and prompts; it does not grant those tools safe authority. Authorization belongs at the tool boundary, using authenticated user and agent identity. An approval should bind the exact action and argument digest, otherwise a resumed workflow can execute something different from what a person reviewed.

Description: Follow one unit of work from left to right. The lower panel is the accounting model. It is intentionally explicit because unmeasured queueing and data movement are the most common reasons that component benchmarks fail to predict production behavior.

The capacity equation

safe autonomy = allowed capability * verified intent * bounded side effect

Treat this as a model to validate, not a constant to copy. Measure each term on the exact hardware, model revision, input distribution, and concurrency regime. Capacity planning should reserve failure headroom; running permanently at the cliff makes recovery impossible when a replica, link, or dependency disappears.

A worked production example

Start with one representative workload and record an end-to-end baseline. Apply the equation safe autonomy = allowed capability * verified intent * bounded side effect using measured—not advertised—rates. Increase concurrency until the first queue grows, then identify whether agent identity, MCP registry, policy engine, or durable workflow owns that queue. The saturation point and recovery curve are more useful than an isolated peak number.

Run the experiment in at least three regimes: one request for floor latency, a realistic concurrency distribution for normal operation, and controlled overload for backpressure and recovery. A system is not healthy merely because it eventually completes every request. Queue age, deadline misses, quality, and resource recovery all belong in the acceptance criteria.

Description: The timeline identifies where work waits and where it executes. Instrument both sides of every transition so queue time cannot be mistaken for compute time. Compare steady state with the warm-up and recovery periods rather than deleting them from the report.

Placement, topology, and scale

Logical architecture hides physical asymmetry. Two workers can have the same configuration while differing in accelerator generation, NUMA path, network hops, cache warmth, storage locality, or noisy-neighbor pressure. Placement must therefore be expressed as constraints and verified through telemetry.

Description: The two domains are intentionally independent. Local queues contain transient pressure; durable identity lets work move; the fabric is treated as a finite resource. A cross-domain design should say what happens when the fabric is slow, partitioned, or only partially available.

Failure analysis

The triggering event is rarely the entire incident. Cascades occur when a local failure creates retries, retries create more load, and overloaded dependencies become less responsive. Bound attempts, preserve the original deadline, add jitter, and open circuits by route or failure domain rather than disabling an entire platform.

Description: Trace the trigger downward into three distinct consequences. Correctness, performance, and operability require different detection and recovery controls; one generic health check cannot represent all three.

The control loop

Production optimization is a feedback system. Signals must be fresh and correctly scoped; decisions need hysteresis or cooldown; actions need bounds; verification must compare the intended metric without hiding regressions elsewhere. If a controller can add load faster than the system can observe the result, it will oscillate.

Description: A safe controller closes the loop. It does not stop after changing a batch size, replica count, route weight, or precision. It checks quality and SLOs, attributes the outcome, and rolls back when the invariant is violated.

What to measure

  • latency and queue time around agent identity
  • capacity and pressure for MCP registry
  • throughput, failures, and retries in policy engine
  • decision reasons emitted by durable workflow
  • quality, cost, and SLO goodput by workload slice

Always segment these measurements by model revision, workload class, hardware type, and outcome. A fleet-wide average can look healthy while one tenant, long-context bucket, adapter, or accelerator generation is failing.

From laboratory result to production capability

A laboratory result proves that one configuration worked once. A production capability proves that the same contract survives concurrency, skew, partial failure, deployment, and rollback. Record the complete experiment envelope: hardware SKU and topology, driver and runtime versions, model and tokenizer digests, request distribution, warm-up policy, concurrency, precision, and every non-default control. Without that envelope, a performance number is not reproducible evidence.

Separate floor latency, sustainable throughput, and recovery capacity. Floor latency is measured with no queue. Sustainable throughput is the highest rate that keeps queue age and SLO violations bounded over a long run. Recovery capacity is spare work the system can absorb after a replica, link, node, or dependency is lost. These are different numbers. Peak throughput is usually above the sustainable point and says little about safe production capacity.

Roll out in stages. First shadow inputs where policy permits, then canary a narrow workload slice, then increase traffic while comparing quality and operational distributions with the baseline. Make the rollback trigger machine-readable before rollout begins. A rollback that requires an operator to rediscover the previous model, state schema, or runtime image is not a rollback plan.

Debugging order

Debug from the outside inward. Confirm the request identity and deadline, then measure admission and queueing, then state lookup or transfer, then execution, then serialization and downstream delivery. Correlate all five with one trace identity. This order prevents a common mistake: optimizing the most visible kernel while the actual delay is a queue, a copy, a collective, a storage read, or a retry outside the profiler window.

Change one independent variable at a time and retain the raw samples. If a change improves the median but damages the p99, quality, or recovery time, it is not an unconditional improvement. Explain which workload segment benefits and encode that scope in routing or policy instead of applying the change globally.

Design-review checklist

  • Is every artifact and state transition bound to a stable version or digest?
  • Where does work wait, what bounds that queue, and what happens at the bound?
  • Which failures are retryable, and how are deadline and idempotency preserved?
  • Which resource saturates first under representative load?
  • Can operators distinguish correctness failure from overload and dependency failure?
  • Does rollback restore both code and state compatibility?
  • Are sensitive inputs, outputs, credentials, and telemetry scoped and redacted?
  • Has the recovery path been tested under partial failure rather than described only on paper?

Primary and official references

  • Model Context Protocol specification
  • OWASP MCP Security
  • OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions

The takeaway

An agent control plane governs identity, tool discovery, intent, approval, execution, receipts, and resumable state while leaving reasoning inside the chosen runtime. The engineering discipline is to make that claim measurable: define the contract, map state and work to real resources, test the failure boundary, and operate a feedback loop that protects correctness before chasing peak throughput.

A 21-part field guide to the hardware, runtimes, distributed systems, serving platforms, data planes, security controls, and operational practices behind production AI.

  1. 01Inside the GPU: From SMs to HBM Without the Hand-Waving
